race.name: Mallorca 312
race.tagline: 312 kilometers around cycling's Mediterranean paradise. Serra de Tramuntana climbs, closed roads, 8,500 riders, and a finish that makes you forget every watt of suffering.

vitals.registration: Opens early October, sells out within minutes. 2026 entry fee EUR 159.99 (includes jersey, timing chip, finisher medal). Waiting list available after sellout.
vitals.prize_purse: None. Trophies for top 3 male/female finishers and age categories. Commemorative medals for all finishers.
vitals.aid_stations: 6 feed stations at approximately km 50, 95, 136, 189, 238, and 284. Each provides food, drinks, toilets, and mechanical assistance.
vitals.cutoff_time: 14 hours 20 minutes (finish by 8:40 PM). Intermediate checkpoints at km 97 (by 11:15 AM) and km 220 (by 4:30 PM).

climate.description: Late April brings 19-22C (66-72F) daytime temperatures with 8+ hours of sunshine. Early morning start at 6:30 AM means cool conditions (11-13C) for the first hours. Serra de Tramuntana mountains run several degrees cooler than the coast. Rain occurs on average 5 days in April but is typically brief.
challenges: Pre-dawn start temperatures around 11-13C require layering
Mountain sections in Serra de Tramuntana can be significantly cooler and exposed to wind
Occasional spring rain showers can make descents treacherous
Late afternoon heat possible on the flat eastern sections after 250km of fatigue

terrain.surface: Well-maintained Spanish tarmac throughout. Fully closed roads with no traffic.
features: Serra de Tramuntana mountain passes with extended switchback descents
Exposed coastal road sections with crosswind potential
Fast descents off Puig Major and Coll de Soller requiring confident handling
Rolling terrain through the eastern flatlands in the second half
Urban sections through villages and towns

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: The benchmark European gran fondo. Sells out in minutes, attracts legends of the sport, and delivers a once-in-a-lifetime circumnavigation of cycling's favorite island.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: 14 criteria sum=56 + cultural_impact=5 = 61. (61/70)*100 = 87. Prestige p5 + score>=75 confirms T1. Road discipline event on fully paved, closed roads.

logistics.explanation: Closed roads for 8,500 riders across an entire island. Palma de Mallorca airport (PMI) is a major European hub with direct flights from virtually every capital, 45 minutes to the start in Playa de Muro. Six fully stocked feed stations with mechanical support. Wave starts manage the enormous field. Registration sells out in minutes, which is logistically frustrating, but on race day the machine runs like clockwork.

length.explanation: 312 kilometers in a single day. That is 194 miles of pedaling, with a 14-hour-20-minute cutoff that tells you exactly how serious the organizers are about the distance. The front runners finish in under 9 hours. Mid-pack riders face 11-13 hours in the saddle. This is not a century ride with a bonus loop. This is a full circumnavigation of an island, and your legs will know it by kilometer 250.

technicality.explanation: Paved roads throughout. No gravel, no singletrack, no navigational challenges. The technical demands are limited to fast descents off Serra de Tramuntana passes where switchbacks and speed combine to demand attention. Coll de Soller and the descent from Puig Major reward confident bike handling, but this is road cycling, not adventure riding. The difficulty is in duration and elevation, not terrain complexity.

elevation.explanation: 5,050 meters of climbing — over 16,500 feet — concentrated almost entirely in the first half of the route through Serra de Tramuntana. Coll de Femenia (7.5km at 6%), Puig Major (14km at 6%, topping out at 889m), and Coll de Soller deliver relentless ascending. The cruel part is that by the time you hit the flatter eastern half, your legs have already been demolished by 3,000+ meters of climbing. The elevation is not alpine, but the cumulative load is savage.

climate.explanation: Late April Mediterranean is generally cooperative — warm days, manageable humidity, low rain probability. But the 6:30 AM start means freezing through the first hour, and Serra de Tramuntana creates its own microclimate that can be 5-8 degrees cooler and significantly windier than the coast. Some years bring perfect sunshine. Others bring mountain rain that turns fast descents into survival exercises. You pack for three seasons and hope you only need one.

altitude.explanation: Puig Major tops out at 889 meters — not high enough for any physiological concern. The start at sea level and the highest point below 900m means zero altitude acclimatization is needed. You will suffer on Puig Major, but from gradient and fatigue, not thin air.

adventure.explanation: You are riding around an entire Mediterranean island in a single day. The route traverses UNESCO-listed Serra de Tramuntana mountains, hugs cliff-edge coastal roads, passes through ancient villages, and returns to the turquoise waters of Playa de Muro. The sense of journey — of having circumnavigated something real and beautiful — elevates this far beyond a typical loop course. It loses a point only because the roads are well-paved and well-supported. This is an adventure, but a civilized one.

prestige.explanation: The most prestigious amateur cycling sportive in Europe, possibly the world. Alberto Contador, Miguel Indurain, Alejandro Valverde, Annemiek van Vleuten, Sean Kelly, Ivan Basso, and Stephen Roche have all ridden it. It sells out 8,500 places in minutes. When you tell a European cyclist you finished the Mallorca 312, the response is immediate respect. This is the Unbound of road cycling — the event that defines the category.

race_quality.explanation: Sixteen editions and counting, with professional race organization that closes every road on the route. Six aid stations, mechanical support, intermediate timing checkpoints, medical coverage, and a finisher infrastructure that handles 8,500 riders without chaos. The included jersey is genuinely good. The timing system works. The volunteer corps is enormous. This is what happens when an event has had over a decade to refine every detail.

experience.explanation: Mallorca is cycling paradise, and this event is the crown jewel. You ride through UNESCO World Heritage landscapes at sunrise. You climb the same passes that professional teams use for January training camps. You descend switchbacks above the Mediterranean with the sea glittering 500 meters below. You finish at sunset on a beach. The post-race atmosphere in Playa de Muro — exhausted riders from 60+ countries sharing stories over cold beer — is what cycling community looks like at its absolute best.

community.explanation: 8,500 riders from over 60 countries. The event has become a pilgrimage for European cycling culture, drawing riders from Germany, UK, Netherlands, Scandinavia, and beyond. The race week atmosphere in Playa de Muro and Port d'Alcudia transforms the north coast into a cycling festival. Local Mallorcan support is genuine — the island's economy depends on cycling tourism, and residents embrace the event. The shared suffering of 312km creates bonds that last.

field_depth.explanation: The sheer size — 8,500 entries — guarantees depth across every ability level. Ex-pros and current professional cyclists add star power. The front group races genuinely hard, with winning times under 9 hours representing serious competitive cycling. But this is fundamentally a sportive, not a race. Most riders are targeting a finish, not a podium. The competitive depth is broad rather than razor-sharp.

value.explanation: EUR 160 gets you closed roads across an entire island, six feed stations, mechanical support, a quality cycling jersey, timing chip, finisher medal, and access to one of cycling's iconic events. For the quality of organization and the scale of the experience, this represents strong value. The challenge is actually getting an entry — the event sells out so fast that the real cost is the stress of refreshing the registration page.

expenses.explanation: Mallorca in late April is peak cycling season. Flights from northern Europe run EUR 150-400. Accommodation in Playa de Muro or Port d'Alcudia during race week commands premium rates — budget EUR 100-200/night minimum. Add bike transport or rental, airport transfers, food for a long race week, and the entry fee, and you are looking at EUR 800-1,500 total for a solo trip. Not cheap, but not outrageous for a destination sportive of this caliber.

biased_opinion.overview:
Mallorca 312 is the event that proved a gran fondo could be as culturally significant as any professional race. It is not the hardest 312 kilometers you will ever ride — the roads are perfect, the support is immaculate, and the Mediterranean provides a backdrop that makes suffering feel almost romantic. But make no mistake: 312 kilometers with 5,050 meters of climbing will find every weakness in your preparation. The front-loaded elevation through Serra de Tramuntana is designed to break you before the halfway mark, and the flat eastern sections that follow are not a gift — they are a test of what you have left after Puig Major has taken everything.

What separates Mallorca 312 from every other gran fondo is the combination of scale and intimacy. Eight thousand five hundred riders sounds like chaos, but the closed-road organization and wave starts create an experience that feels personal. You climb alongside riders from 60 countries, share feed stations with people whose names you will never know but whose suffering you understand completely, and cross the finish line on a beach at sunset knowing you just rode around an entire island. Alberto Contador has done this ride. Miguel Indurain has done this ride. And when you finish, you understand exactly why they keep coming back.

The only honest criticism is access. The event sells out in minutes, turning registration day into its own competitive event. If you get an entry, do not waste it. Train for the distance, respect the climbs, pack layers for the mountains, and arrive in Mallorca early enough to shake out your legs on the same roads you will race. This is one of the great single-day cycling experiences on the planet, and it earns that reputation every April.

final_verdict.one_liner: The defining European gran fondo — 312km around cycling's most beloved island on fully closed roads.
final_verdict.should_you_race: If you have ever dreamed of riding around an entire Mediterranean island in a single day, this is the one. You need a genuine endurance base — 312km with 5,000m of climbing is not a stretch goal you wing on enthusiasm. Train for 6+ hour rides, respect the Serra de Tramuntana climbs, and register the moment entries open because you will not get a second chance. This event is worth traveling for, worth training for, and worth the suffering.
alternatives: L'Etape du Tour (France) — similar prestige, iconic climbs, but shorter distance
Maratona dles Dolomites (Italy) — harder climbing, smaller field, lottery entry
Marmotte Granfondo Alpes (France) — more extreme elevation, col-hunting format
Gran Fondo Stelvio Santini (Italy) — alpine climbing focus, stunning scenery

logistics.nearest_airport: Palma de Mallorca (PMI) — major European hub, 45-60 minutes to Playa de Muro by car or transfer
logistics.travel_tips: Book flights early — Mallorca in late April is peak cycling season. Airport transfers available through race organizer partnerships with OK Mobility. TIB bus from Palma to Alcudia takes approximately 75 minutes. Car rental recommended for flexibility during race week. Bike boxes can be stored at most hotels. Low-cost carriers (Ryanair, EasyJet, Eurowings) serve Palma from most European cities.
logistics.accommodation: Playa de Muro is the start/finish — stay within 5km for race morning convenience. Official race hotels include Vanity Hotel Golf, Sunrise Aparthotel, and Eden Lago. Port d'Alcudia offers more options 10 minutes away. Book 6+ months in advance as race week accommodation sells out nearly as fast as entries. Tour operator packages (Sportive Breaks, Love Velo, Sports Tours International) bundle entry, hotel, and transfers.
logistics.gear_recommendations: Road bike with compact gearing (34/32 minimum for Serra de Tramuntana gradients). Arm warmers and gilet essential for 6:30 AM start and mountain sections. Two bottles minimum between aid stations. Pockets loaded with gels and bars — 312km demands 300-400g of carbohydrates. Rain jacket strongly recommended as mountain weather can change rapidly. GPS tracker rental available from organizers for EUR 15.

notable_editions: 2010: First edition with 199 riders on a hot June day, almost all from Mallorca and mainland Spain plus three Irishmen. Born from a dinner conversation at Cafe Almudaina in Arta in 2008.
2016: Route changed from full coastal circumnavigation to current Serra de Tramuntana mountain loop format for better organization and road closures.
2022: 12th edition featured legendary lineup including Alberto Contador, Miguel Indurain, Ivan Basso, Sean Kelly, and Joseba Beloki riding alongside 8,000 participants.

2024: 14th edition with 8,500 sold-out entries. Roman Locher (Czech Republic) set men's course record of 8:51:13. Sylvia Hermans (Belgium) won women's 312km in 9:59:15. Registration sold out in 6 minutes.
2026: 16th edition — 8,500 entries sold out instantly when registration opened October 7, 2025.
history.records: Men's 312km course record: 8:51:13 by Roman Locher (2024). Women's 312km record: 9:59:15 by Sylvia Hermans (2024). Growth from 199 riders in 2010 to 8,500 in 2024 — a 42x increase in 14 years.
history.growth: Founded in 2010 by Xisco Lliteras after a conversation among local cyclists in Cafe Almudaina in Arta in 2008. The idea was simple: ride around the entire island in one day. From 199 riders in the inaugural edition to 8,500 selling out in minutes, Mallorca 312 has become the most sought-after sportive entry in European cycling. Notable riders include Miguel Indurain, Alberto Contador, Alejandro Valverde, Annemiek van Vleuten, Sean Kelly, Ivan Basso, Stephen Roche, Joseba Beloki, Maurizio Fondriest, Pedro Horrillo, and Haimar Zubeldia. Title sponsor OK Mobility provides the commercial backbone for closed-road logistics across the island.

course_description.overview: A 312km circumnavigation of Mallorca starting and finishing at Playa de Muro on the northeast coast. The route heads north to Port de Pollenca before turning into Serra de Tramuntana for the major climbing in the first half. After descending to the southwest coast via Andratx, it crosses through the interior and eastern flatlands to return home. Nearly all 5,050m of climbing is front-loaded in the first 160km, leaving the second half as a war of attrition on tired legs across rolling terrain.

segments.name: Playa de Muro to Port de Pollenca
segments.description: Flat coastal warm-up along the Bay of Alcudia. Riders settle into groups as the sun rises over the Mediterranean. Enjoy it — this is the last flat section you will see for 130km.

segments.name: Coll de Femenia
segments.description: The first real test. 7.5km at an average 6% gradient with 405m of elevation gain on the MA-10 heading southwest from Pollenca into Serra de Tramuntana. A proper warm-up for what is about to come.

segments.name: Serra de Tramuntana traverse
segments.description: The heart of the route. Continuous climbing and descending through UNESCO World Heritage mountain landscapes. The road clings to cliff edges above the Mediterranean with drops that demand respect. Tunnels, switchbacks, and views that make you forget the pain — briefly.
segments.terrain_detail: Mountain roads, mixed gradients, technical descents

segments.name: Puig Major
segments.description: The queen stage. 14km at an average 6% with the final 3km averaging over 7%. Puig Major tops out at 889m — the highest point on the route and the longest sustained climb of the day. The Monnaber tunnel at the summit marks the beginning of the end of the major climbing.

segments.name: Coll de Soller and descent to southwest coast
segments.description: The iconic switchbacks of Coll de Soller followed by a fast descent toward Andratx and the southwest coast. This section rewards confident descending — the hairpins are tight and the roads are fast. Feed station at km 136 arrives just when you need it most.
segments.terrain_detail: Mountain pass with technical descent, switchback hairpins

segments.name: Southwest coast to interior
segments.description: Undulating terrain transitioning from the western mountains to the central plain. The major climbing is behind you, but 200km into a 312km ride, every false flat feels like a wall. The landscape shifts from dramatic coastline to agricultural interior.
segments.terrain_detail: Rolling hills, mixed terrain, agricultural roads

segments.name: Eastern flatlands
segments.description: The psychological crucible. Flat to gently rolling roads through the eastern interior. No dramatic scenery to distract you. No descents to recover on. Just the relentless accumulation of kilometers on legs that stopped cooperating 50km ago. Wind exposure is the primary threat here.
segments.terrain_detail: Flat to rolling, exposed to crosswinds

segments.name: Return to Playa de Muro
segments.description: The final 32km back to the coast. The Bay of Alcudia appears in the distance and the finish line becomes real. Riders who have been grinding alone for hours suddenly find energy they did not know existed. The finish on the beachfront at Playa de Muro at sunset is one of cycling's great moments.
segments.terrain_detail: Flat coastal road, sea level approach to finish

key_features: Front-loaded climbing — 5,050m of elevation in the first 160km
Coll de Femenia (7.5km, 6%) as the opening salvo
UNESCO World Heritage Serra de Tramuntana traverse
312km on fully closed roads — no traffic, no interruptions
6 fully stocked feed stations with mechanical support
course_description.strategy: Respect the front-loaded climbing. The temptation to ride hard on the flat opening 25km is enormous — 8,500 riders and adrenaline create a pace that will destroy you by Puig Major. Ride the first 100km at 70-75% of threshold. Feed aggressively at every aid station. Save one gear for the eastern flatlands where the wind and mental fatigue combine to break riders who went too hard in the mountains. Your race starts at km 200.

race_challenges: Extreme distance requiring 10-14 hours of sustained effort
5,050m of climbing front-loaded in the first half
Mountain weather variability in Serra de Tramuntana
Wind exposure on flat eastern sections after 200km of fatigue

non_negotiables.requirement: Multiple 6+ hour endurance rides completed before race day
non_negotiables.why: 312km demands a genuine endurance base. You cannot bluff 194 miles. If your longest ride is 4 hours, you are not ready.

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained climbing preparation with back-to-back mountain efforts
non_negotiables.why: 5,050m of front-loaded climbing requires climbing-specific fitness. Flat riders who can hold power on the plains will crack on Puig Major.

non_negotiables.requirement: Fueling strategy tested and refined on training rides of 10+ hours
non_negotiables.why: 312km requires 300-400g of carbohydrates. Gut training is essential. Discovering your stomach rejects gels at km 200 is a race-ending problem.

videos.title: 2024 edition of Mallorca 312
videos.description:
The fourteenth edition of the Mallorca 312 | OK Mobility gran fondo got underway at Playa de Muro, Mallorca, Spain, on April 27, 2024.

Unlike in 2023, when extremely hot conditions were experienced, the peloton woke up to more pleasant temperatures and cloudy skies. Although, later on in the day, strong winds became the enemy of many of the participants of the 167km, 225km and 312km races.

videos.title: Mallorca 312 M312 Granfondo 2025  - Die 167km Strecke im Rennvideo
videos.description:
Hier gibt meinen Rennbericht zum Mallorca 312 Granfondo. Es geht über die 167km, 2500hm "Sprintstrecke" von Playa de Muro durch das Traumuntana Gebirge und zurück. 


#Mallorca #M312 #Granfondo #Racevideo # Rennbericht #Rennrad #Marathon #PlayaDeMuro

videos.title: Mallorca 312 Granfondo 2024 - 167km Runde
videos.description:
Ein Zusammenschnitt der 167km Runde des Radrennen Mallorca 312 am 27.04.2024 mit Start und Ziel in Playa de Muro. Neben der großen 312km Runde gibt es noch die kürzere 225km und die "ganz kleine" Runde mit 167km welche etwa 2800 Höhenmeter hat und bis nach Esporles auf der 312km Runde verläuft dann jedoch über Llosetta und Sa Pobla zurück nach Playa de Muro führt.

#mallorca #312 #mallorca312 #radrennen #rennrad #alcudia #granfondo #Fahrrad
videos.curation_reason: Recent 2024 race recap with good production quality showing the 167km course through the Tramuntana mountains.

quotes.text: The first four hours in the mountains is always very hard - you start with 50 people on the first climb and then it goes down to 40, 30, and we came out to the flats like seven people and then rode for the last 150K just three people in the wind that was very hard.

key_challenges.name: The First Climb
key_challenges.description: The beginning of the first climb causes major attrition - field drops from 50 riders to 40 then 30 people very quickly.

key_challenges.name: Mountain Section
key_challenges.description: First four hours in the mountains described as always very hard, causing the field to whittle down to just seven riders.

key_challenges.name: Final 150K Flats
key_challenges.description: Last 150K ridden with just three people battling constant wind, described as very hard.

terrain_notes.text: Race features significant wind exposure on the flats, requiring constant effort even in small groups.

race_day_tips.text: Expect major field reduction in the mountains - be prepared for the race to split dramatically in the first four hours.
Race starts with a neutralized pace but becomes very hard once hitting the mountains.

additional_quotes.text: It's a 9 hour race, always very long. It starts off easy with a neutralized pace but then the first four hours in the mountains is always very hard.

rider_intel.search_text: The Mallorca 312 is described as a grueling 9-hour endurance test that begins with a neutralized start before becoming extremely challenging. The race features a brutal mountain section in the first four hours that causes dramatic field reduction, with groups dropping from 50 riders to just 7 survivors. The first climb is particularly selective, quickly eliminating riders. After the mountains, riders face 150 kilometers of wind-exposed flats that require constant effort even in small groups. The combination of sustained climbing followed by wind-battered flats creates a true test of endurance, with only the strongest riders surviving to contest the finish.
